---
title: "04_Biological_Data"
author: "Michael Tassia"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
---

## JHU Biology REU 2024 - Analyzing and visualizing data in genetics

In this lesson, we'll continue practicing with `tidyverse` tools to visualize common genetic data formats. Figure reproduction using diverse data sets serves as valuable practice with data literacy and familiarity with `R`.

We'll work with the following data:

1.  [Genotype data from the 1000 Genomes Project](https://www.internationalgenome.org/)
2.  [Genome-wide association study data from Pan-UK Biobank](https://pan.ukbb.broadinstitute.org/)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Genetic diversity in the 1000 Genomes Project

#### Allele frequency spectrum (AFS)

In the first section of today's lesson, we'll work with a subset of the genotype data from the [1000 Genomes Project](https://www.internationalgenome.org/) (1KGP). The overarching goal of the 1000 Genomes Project is to capture and describe genetic variation across a diverse cohort of human ancestries. We can visualize the global distribution of genetic variants using the [Geography of Genetic Variants browser](https://popgen.uchicago.edu/ggv/?data=%221000genomes%22&chr=1&pos=222087833). Since the project's [first publication in 2010](https://www.nature.com/articles/nature09534), the data set has grown and motivated additional studies that aimed to quantify genetic variance beyond short variants (i.e., single nucleotide polymorphisms and short insertions/deletions). If you want to learn more about the 1KGP data set, here are a few papers:

-   [Sankararaman S. *et al.* 2014. The genomic landscape of Neanderthal ancestry in present-day humans. *Nature* **507**: 354-357](https://www.nature.com/articles/nature12961)

-   [The 1000 Genomes Project Consortium. 2015. A global reference for human genetic variation, *Nature* **526:** 68-74](https://www.nature.com/articles/nature15393)

-   [Taylor et al. 2023. Sources of gene expression variation in a globally diverse human cohort. *BioRxiv.* https://doi.org/10.1101/2023.11.04.565639](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2023.11.04.565639v4)

In the first section of this lesson, we'll use a subset of the 1KGP data to look at two common plotting formats for visualizing genetic variation in populations: *Allele frequency spectra* and *principle component analysis*.

To begin, let's load and describe the data set:

```{r, results=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

library(tidyverse)
df <- read_csv(file="1KGP_chr21.AFs.csv.gz")

head(df)
```

In this subset of the 1KGP data set, we have aggregated *variant allele frequency* (VAF) measurements from chromosome 21 at two scales:

1.  "Global" VAF (`AF`)
2.  Continental group VAF (`AFR_AF`,`EUR_AF`,`SAS_AF`,`EAS_AF`,`AMR_AF)`

Before we begin plotting, let's describe VAF: VAF is a measurement of the number of *variant* genotypes divided by the total number of genotypes in our sample cohort. Thus, it is a proportion bound between 0 and 1. But what is a *variant* genotype?

When working with genetic sequencing data, we often work with a *reference* - a sample that is used as the baseline with which we make comparisons. In this case, our *reference* is a genome called `GRCh38`. To compute VAF in our data set above, each individual's genome (each *sample* in 1KGP) is compared to the *reference* genome to identify genomic positions (i.e., loci) that that sample's allele is different from the *reference*. Then, by aggregating the count of *variant* samples, we can compute VAF both globally (`AF` column) or within continental groups (e.g., `AFR_AF` column).

Returning to our data, how can we visualize and explore genetic variation? Let's start by working through the following exercise to plot a histogram of allele frequency. In genetics, we call this kind of histogram an *allele frequency spectrum*.

```{r}

# Using the `AF` variable, plot a histogram using `ggplot` and a `histogram` geom with `binwidth = 0.01`

```

In the plot we generated above, we saw that most variants are observed at very low frequency (0-5%) when we look at the "global" trend in genetic variation. What about the trends *within* each continental group?

```{r}

# First we need to use `pivot_longer()` to transform our dataset for plotting.

df<-df %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols=3:7,
               names_to = "ContGroup",
               values_to="GroupVAF") %>%
  select(!AF)

# Now that we have our data frame in a format compatible for plotting, let's plot the AFS for each continental group. Here, use `facet_wrap(~ContGroup,nrow=5)` to plot each continental group on its own.

```

From these plots, we can see that the distributions do not look qualitatively different from one another - most variants are very low frequency. Biologically, this pattern is consistent with recent population expansions. We won't dwell on it during this workshop, but you can learn more about how an AFS can provide insight into the [historical evolutionary pressures that shape populations of species in this paper](https://www.nature.com/articles/nrg4005).

To contrast our observations with human population genetics, we can also plot an AFS under neutral evolutionary pressures (again, take a look at the paper linked above for more biological discourse on "neutral evolutionary pressures"). To look at neutral trends, we will simulate some data to plot:

```{r}

df<-tibble(AF=seq(0.01,1,by=0.01)) %>%
  mutate(Prop=0.01/AF)
               
ggplot(df,aes(x=AF,y=Prop))+
         geom_col(position="identity")+
         theme_classic()
```

Although we don't discuss the biological details of the theoretical AFS here, the plotting exercise above serves as an example for how we can use simulated data to generate expectations and contrast them with our observations. In addition to statistical tests, visualization/plotting is a powerful tool for exploring data and hypothesis testing. In the next section, we'll explore another common format of data exploration in genetics: *principle component analysis*.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Genetic diversity in the 1000 Genomes Project

#### Principle component analysis (PCA)

As a reminder, we compute *variant allele frequency* by first *genotyping* each sample - measuring the alleles in each individual's genome relative to a reference genome (i.e., `GRCh38` in our data set). In addition to using AFS to gain insight into the whole population, we can also compare individual genotypes to measure relationships between samples. Though there are many ways to investigate relationships between samples (e.g., phylogenetics), we'll apply *principle component analysis* (PCA).

PCA can be complex to describe; but in essence, PCA is a data transformation procedure that aims to simplify multi-dimensional data (e.g., data that can be described ≥3 axes) while preserving patterns of shared variance. The main output from PCA are *principle components* (PCs), where each PC is effectively a "new axis of variation" that describe our data. [Here's a link to an interactive example of what PCA is and how it works](https://setosa.io/ev/principal-component-analysis/).

In the example below, we'll use PCA to explore the genetic relationships between 1KGP samples (using common variants on chromosome 21). For this example, we'll use genotype data from a random subset (500 loci) of chromosome 21. This subset will include only *common variants* (VAF ≥ 5%), as rare variants have very little "shared information" to help us dissect relationships between samples.

The code below will also revisit many functions and data transformation procedures that we've covered throughout this workshop. This will be the most complex example we've encountered so far, so work slowly and carefully to understand each step.

```{r, results=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

# LOAD DATA 

df <- read_csv(file="1KGP_chr21_genotypes.csv.gz")
df.meta <- read_tsv(file="1KGP_metadata.tsv")

head(df)
head(df.meta)
```

Using the data we've loaded above, we want to do the following:

1.  [Prepare]{.underline} `df` for PCA (we'll be using the `prcomp()` ***function*** to perform PCA). `prcomp()` expects a `matrix` ***object*** where rows are samples (e.g., *HG00096*) and columns are variables (e.g. *chr21_7924894*).
2.  [Perform PCA]{.underline} to quantify patterns of variation among our sample genotypes
3.  [Plot]{.underline} these results to explore patterns of variation

First, we need to prepare `df` for PCA:

```{r}

# PREPARE DATA FOR PCA
# The most common function for PCA is a function called `prcomp` which requires a `matrix` object where the rows are samples and the columns are dimensions (variables). Here, we'll transform our dataset to fit this requirement using a combination of tools we've already learned and a couple new methods.

# Remove columns we don't need (columns 2:7)

# Convert tibble to matrix: tibbles don't store row names and a matrix input to PCA expects both column and row names

# We use the `t()` function to transpose our matrix (rotating 90deg) to use as input for `prcomp`.

```

In the code block above, we performed a series of subsetting (i.e., `select()`), ***object*** conversion (i.e., `as.matrix()`), and reformatting (i.e., `t()`) to convert our information-rich ***data frame*** into a format compatible with our data analysis objectives.

With our properly formatted data in-hand, we can now perform PCA and prepare the output for plotting.

```{r}

# PERFORM PCA AND PREPARE OUTPUT FOR PLOTTING 

pca <- prcomp(df) # `prcomp()` performs the PCA on the matrix

glimpse(pca) # `glimpse()` is another method of looking at an object (part of `dplyr`)

# Although there is a lot of data included in the `prcomp()` output, we're only interested in the principle components stored in `pca$x`. 

pca_results<-pca$x

# This subset of the `prcomp()` output object contains our new variables which capture the relationships between samples, where PC1 explains the most variation in our dataset, PC2 the next greatest, then PC3, and so on (to PC500). Here, we'll use the first two PCs since they capture the most variation (a common starting point for visualizing PCA).

pca_df<-data.frame(sample = rownames(df),
                   PC1 = pca_results[,1], # We use the `[]` operator to subset matrices...
                   PC2 = pca_results[,2]) # ...using the same syntax as data frames.

head(pca_df)

# The final step before plotting is to merge our PCA output with our sample metadata. The reason for this will become clear once we begin plotting. Here, we'll use the `merge()` command.

pca_df<-merge(pca_df,
              df.meta) # By default, `merge` will combine data frames by a shared column name (in this case, "sample")

head(pca_df)
```

We've now generated the PCA results and prepared the output for plotting. In the example above, we also used the `$` in a slightly different way than when we've used it to pull columns from a ***data frame***. Broadly, `$` in R is used to access "subsets/slots" of an object, where the "subset/slot" is defined by the object type (for ***data frames***, it's columns). We won't encounter more varieties of the `$` operator in this workshop, but you will likely encounter `$` for other non-***data-frame*** objects in the future if you continue to use `R`.

Finally, let's return to plotting our exploratory PCA data. We'll work through plotting using a couple exercises:

```{r}

# PLOTTING

# Using the `pca_df` as input, create a scatter plot where PC1 and PC2 are the x- and y-axes, respectively.

```

Using this version of the PCA plot, we observe two things:

1.  PC1 separates one cluster from other clusters along a continuum of samples (points)
2.  PC2 separates the "other" clusters along another continuum

To interpret these axes, however, we need to annotate our data with additional information. First, we'll use sex:

```{r}

# Using the same plotting backbone you have above, color points by `sex`. 


# Natively, the self-reported `sex` variable is being treated as a "continuous" variable; however, our data encodes `sex` as either 1 or 2 - thus it should be treated as "categorical" to reflect the method of data collection. To accommodate this, we can plot `sex` using `as.factor(sex)`; try that below:

```

In this plot, we see that `sex` is evenly distributed across all clusters when plotted with PC1 and PC2 - thus we can conclude that first two PCs are not capturing the variance due to `sex`. Instead, let's now color points by continental group (`contgroup`):

```{r}

# Using the same plotting backbone, color points by `congroup` instead of `sex`. 

```

Using the `contgroup` categorical variable, we can now see that PC1 and PC2 can sufficiently separate the five "continental groups", though there is still a continuum of relationships along each PC. This is because "continental group" labels do not accurately capture biology, but rather reflect social constructs we use to artificially categorize populations. If you'd like to know more about how human geneticists treat population labels, you can [read more about it here](https://nap.nationalacademies.org/resource/26902/interactive/). Although we won't discuss how to compute it here, PC1 and PC2 cumulatively only capture approximately 14% of all variance measured across genotypes - this means that 86% of genetic variance is [*not*]{.underline} explained by "continental group" labels.

To summarize our findings in this section, we examined genetic data from 1KGP to look at patterns of allele sharing on chromosome 22. By plotting *variant allele frequency* as a histogram, we saw that most genetic variants are rare - consistent with an evolutionary history of recent population expansion. Then we used *principle component analysis* to investigate the relationships between samples in 1KGP, showing that the first two *principle components* separate the five "continental groups" (though only explain a fraction of all genetic variance observed). Although we only looked at a single chromosome (the shortest autosome), the trends observed in this section also apply across the whole genome. In the next, and final section, we'll turn our attention to functional genomics.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Genome-wide association studies in the Pan-UK Biobank

#### Manhattan plots

In the section above, we used genotype data from a globally diverse cohort to examine the history of human evolution (and describe modern populations). We can also leverage human genetic diversity to identify genetic loci correlated with a trait.

*Genome-wide association studies* (GWAS) are a type of functional genomic assay that tests for a correlation between a phenotype (e.g., height, resting pulse, or risk of a disease) with a genetic variant. We won't discuss the details underlying the statistical tests behind GWAS; but if you'd like to learn more, please take a look at the [2021 *Nature Reviews* article by Uffelmann *et al*.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s43586-021-00056-9).

For this exercise, we'll use data from the [Pan-UK Biobank](https://pan.ukbb.broadinstitute.org/) to generate Manhattan plots - a common visualization format for representing the genomic landscape of trait associations. The Pan-UK Biobank is a human genetics data resource that aggregates both genotype and health-related data from \~500,000 individuals across the UK of diverse ancestry. By combining the genotype and health-related data, the Pan-UK Biobank performed a GWAS for over [7,000 traits (phenotypes)](https://pan.ukbb.broadinstitute.org/phenotypes) and the data is publicly accessible.

We'll begin by working through an example GWAS for LDL cholesterol, a blood biochemistry measurement that is associated with increased risk for coronary artery disease and stroke. Here, we'll work together to reproduce a pre-generated Manhattan plot before turning to an independent exercise.

![](LDL_GWAS_example.png)

Just as in the first plotting exercise, let's describe the quantitative elements of the plot:

1.  *x-axis* is *position* (labeled by chromosome only)
2.  *y-axis* is the association *p-value* (`-log10` transformed for plotting)
3.  *values* are being plotted as a scatter plot
4.  Two horizontal dotted lines associated with two *statistical significance cutoffs* (we'll discuss this more below)

Now that we have our axes defined, let's look at the data:

```{r, results=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

df <- read_csv(file="PanUKB_LDL_subset.csv.gz")
head(df)
```

This version of the Pan-UK Biobank data is simplified relative to what is downloaded directly from their web portal but still has everything we need to reproduce the plot above. While the first four columns of our ***data frame*** are directly interpretable (*chromosome, position, reference allele,* and *alternative* allele, respectively), the `neglog10_pval_meta` column requires some explanation:

| When testing for an association between a genetic variant and a trait, we perform many statistical tests that produce a *p-value* - a statistic that is bound by 0 and 1. There is a lot of discourse on *p-values* in data science/statistics, and you can [read more about it here](https://www.nature.com/articles/ejhg2015269). For our purposes, we'll use a pragmatic definition for *p-value*: "The probability of the observations, given the null hypothesis." Thus, lower *p-values* suggest greater confidence in the observed result/trend. For visualization purposes, we transform *p-values* with `-log10()`. The following plot shows why:

```{r}

# Simulate p-values between 1 and 1e-300
p_df <- data.frame(idx = 0:300,
                   p = 10^(-(0:300)))

head(p_df)

```

```{r}
# Plot raw p-values
ggplot(data = p_df,
       mapping = aes(x=idx,y=p)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_classic()

# Plot -log10(p-values)
ggplot(data = p_df,
       mapping = aes(x=idx,y=-log10(p))) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_classic()
```

We've described *p-values* (and by extension, the y-axis of our Manhattan plot); let's return to our plotting example. We'll start by plotting on the x- and y-axes, then introduce other elements. This will be the most complex plot we produce during the workshop, so we'll work carefully layer-by-layer:

```{r, results=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

# Plot `pos` on x-axis, `neglog10_pval_meta` on y-axis, and facet by chromosome (`pos` values can be repeated on each chromosome!). Within `facet_wrap()`, use the `ncol` argument to specify the number of facets to present as columns.


  
```

Using only a few lines of code, we made a lot of progress towards reproducing the example Manhattan plot above. However, there are still a few key elements that are missing in our plot. Let's look at the figure again and identify differences that affect interpretation:

![](LDL_GWAS_example.png)

In our plot:

1.  `facets` are out of order
2.  `y-axis` upper-bound is too high
3.  `x-axis` labels are incomprehensible
4.  Dotted lines for p-value significance thresholds are missing

We'll tackle these in order:

```{r, results=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

# To order our `chr` variable, we will use `mutate()` to convert this column into a `factor`. Using `factors` we can specify the order that chromosome values should appear in.

df$chr %>% 
  unique() # Allows us to print the values that occupy the `chr` column

chrs <- c(1:22,"X") # Create a vector listing 1 to 22, then followed by "X"

df <- df %>%
  mutate(chr = factor( x = df$chr, levels = chrs)) # Use the `factor()` function to order the "levels" of df$chr according to our `chrs` vector

# Now that we've re-factored our `chrs` variable, plot using the same plotting command as above:


```

Using `factor()`, we changed our `chr` variable from unorganized *`character`* variable, to an ordered *`factor`* variable. This is a very common procedure when plotting categorical variables that natively have an order (e.g., months of the year).

Next, we'll address the *y-axis* bounds and the *x-axis* labels:

```{r, results=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

# Adjust the y-axis bounds


```

```{r}
# Remove the noisy x-axis text


```

```{r}
# Make a few clarity changes: Remove x-axis features, add axis labels, move facet labels to the bottom (`strip.position="bottom"` in `facet_wrap`)


```

Now that we've organized the axes and made a few style adjustments, we can add the last major plotting feature: significance threshold lines. In the example plot, there are two dotted lines:

1.  Black dashed line: "Standard" significance threshold (0.05)
2.  Red dashed line: "Default" GWAS significance threshold (5e-8)

![](LDL_GWAS_example.png)

We can add these two lines (after applying a `-log10` transform so they can be plotted on the same scale as our p-values) using `geom_hline()`:

```{r, results=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

# Add `geom_hline` on top of the scatter plot layer


```

With that addition, the Manhattan plot we've assembled is quantitatively the same as the example we were trying to recreate! There are, however, a few more cosmetic modifications that are worth highlighting (though they do not change our results, these changes may help with ease of interpretation):

1.  The points are large and overlapping (this is called *overplotting*)
2.  We have large gaps between chromosome columns
3.  Using colors, we can better distinguish between adjacent chromosomes.

We'll address these points below:

```{r, results=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

# To address points 1 and 2, we'll pivot from `facet_wrap` to `facet_grid` which gives a little more control to spacing when we have prior knowledge on the number of facets we have and how they're organized

#The following `facet_grid` command will address spacing issues:
#  facet_grid(cols=vars(chr), # Create a column facet for each chr
#             switch = "x", # Place facet label on bottom
#             space = "free_x", # Allow facets to be different widths
#             scales = "free_x") + # Allow chrs to have different bounds



```

```{r}

# Finally, to use alternating colors (a common feature of Manhattan plots), we'll need to create a color palette and use `scale_color_manual()`

cols <- c(rep(x = c("blue","orange"),times = 11),"blue") # `rep()` will repeat a pattern some number of `times` and output a vector. We add an additional "blue" for the X chromosome.


```

Though there are some minor differences between the two versions of the plot (due some upstream data-subsetting performed for this workshop), we've faithfully reproduced the plot showing the same result! While we could make some further modifications, this example serves as a scaffold for which Manhattan plots can be built from the ground up. Biologically, we observe the strongest signal for high *LDL Cholesterol* at the end of chromosome 6. This GWAS peak is in the gene *LPA* which is fundamental to plasma lipoprotein assembly.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

As a final exercise to this lesson (and the workshop as a whole), we will use the blood glucose GWAS result from the Pan-UK Biobank:

```{r, results=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

df <- read_csv(file="PanUKB_Glu_subset.csv.gz")
head(df)
```

With these data, practice data preparation and plotting by performing the following tasks:

```{r, results=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

#   1. Remove the `bonfp` column using `select()` from `dplyr`
#   2. Filter the data frame to only chromosomes 1 through 5 (you can use the following expression `chr %in% c(1:5)` with `filter()` to test whether `chr` is any value in 1:5)
#   3. Create a Manhattan plot for your filtered data frame where:
#     a. The `color` aes is scaled by chromosome
#     b. Include horizontal lines for significance cutoff(s)
#     c. Readily interpretable (e.g., fix axis labels & aesthetic changes)
#   4. Filter the data frame to include only the chromosome with the highest p-value
#   5. Plot the Manhattan plot for only this chromosome
#   6. Using a similar method as `scale_y_continuous(limits=c(0,100))` that we used in the example above, use `scale_x_continuous(limits=...)` to zoom into the strongest hit on the chromosome (this may take several iterated attempts)

```

Unlike the LDL GWAS, there are many significant hits for loci that affect blood glucose biochemistry. Although GWAS is a powerful tool to identify genetic associations with a trait, these analyses often open more questions than they answer - ones that require further scientific rigor to understand the mechanisms underlying the association.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## And that's the JHU Biology REU data science workshop!

We've covered a lot in the past three days of the workshop, starting from introducing `R` and ending with reproducing some plots common in genetic research. Though we don't expect you to memorize or be exceptionally proficient now, you can use this workshop as a resource to familiarize yourself with common methods for analyzing biological data.

Computational skills (in `R`, `python`, `bash`, or other coding languages) will provide an invaluable (and versatile) toolkit for your own research. As a reminder, the following resources are available freely online should you want to continue practicing:

-   [Hands-On Programming with R](https://rstudio-education.github.io/hopr/)
-   [R for Data Science (2e)](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/)
